1: ʾôdeh YHWH bĕkol-libbî (ʾōḏeh YHWH bĕḵol-libbî): 'I will praise the LORD with my whole heart.' The qal imperfect ʾôdeh (from yādâ, 'to praise/confess') expresses votive resolve. The superscription assigns the psalm to David and connects it to ʿal-mût labbēn — a phrase of uncertain meaning (possibly 'death of a son' or a melody title). The pairing of Psalms 9–10 as a single incomplete acrostic (the two share contiguous Hebrew letters ʾālep–kāp) suggests original unity; scribal or liturgical tradition divided them.
2: ʾeśmĕḥâ wĕʾeʿlōs bĕkā (ʾeśmĕḥâ wĕʾeʿlōs bĕḵā): 'I will be glad and exult in you.' Two cohortative forms — śāmaḥ (gladness) and ʿālas (exulting/leaping for joy) — express the emotional fullness of praise. The preposition bĕ- ('in you') grounds the joy in YHWH's person, not merely his acts. The phrase ʾăzammĕrâ šimkā 'elyôn, 'I will sing praises to your name, O Most High,' introduces the divine title ʿelyôn (Most High), associated with Melchizedek's blessing in Genesis 14:18-20 and used especially in Psalms for YHWH's universal sovereignty.
3: bĕšûb ʾôyĕbay ʾāḥôr (bĕšûḇ ʾōyĕḇay ʾāḥôr): 'when my enemies turn back.' The infinitive construct bĕšûb introduces a temporal clause: the psalmist's praise is grounded in past experience of enemies' flight. The root kāšal ('stumble/fall') describes their collapse; yōbĕdû ('they perished') is a niphal perfect — passive voice capturing finality. The presence of YHWH is the cause: mippānêkā ('from before your face'), a recurring Psalter motif where the divine countenance both repels enemies and sustains the righteous.
4: kî ʿāśîtā mišpāṭî wĕdînî (kî ʿāśîṯā mišpāṭî wĕḏînî): 'for you have maintained my judgment and my cause.' The paired nouns mišpāṭ and dîn both designate legal vindication; together they constitute a hendiadys — 'my just cause.' The verb ʿāśâ is a perfect of completed action: YHWH has already acted as judge. The image of yāšabtā lĕkissēʾ, 'you sat down to the throne,' pictures YHWH enthroned as cosmic judge — language that anticipates the heavenly court scenes of Dan 7 and Rev 4–5.
5: gāʿartā gôyim ʾibbadtā rāšāʿ (gāʿartā gôyim ʾibbadtā rāšāʿ): 'you have rebuked the nations, destroyed the wicked.' The verb gāʿar ('rebuke with authority') is the same verb used in Psalms 18:15 and Psalms 106:9 for YHWH's rebuke of sea and nations — a term of theophanic power. Paired with ʾibbadtā (piel perfect of ʾābad, 'to cause to perish'), the two verbs are a tight synonymous parallel: rebuke leads to destruction. The blotting of the wicked man's name — šĕmām māḥîtā — echoes the covenant curse of obliterating remembrance (cf. Deuteronomy 9:14).
6: ḥorbôt tammû lānĕṣaḥ (ḥorbôṯ tammû lānĕṣaḥ): 'their ruins have come to a perpetual end.' The noun ḥorbôt (ruins, desolations) is plural and emphatic: entire cities reduced to waste. The phrase tammû lānĕṣaḥ — 'ended forever' — uses lānĕṣaḥ, a word meaning 'to perpetuity/to victory,' which in Psalms often signals eschatological finality. The enemy is addressed in second person in the Hebrew: 'O enemy — finished, ruins forever!' This apostrophe sharpens the rhetorical force of the vindication.
7: YHWH lĕʿôlām yēšēb (YHWH lĕʿôlām yēšēḇ): 'the LORD sits enthroned forever.' Against the transience of the wicked (Psalms 9:6), YHWH's eternal session on the throne provides the structural antithesis. The verb yāšab ('sit/be enthroned') is used for royal stability; konnēn lammišpāṭ kissēʾô, 'he has established his throne for justice,' adds that the throne's purpose is mišpāṭ — not raw power but righteous governance. The phrase anticipates Ps 93–99, the enthronement psalms.
8: wĕhûʾ yišpōṭ-tēbēl bĕṣedeq (wĕhûʾ yišpōṭ-tēḇēl bĕṣeḏeq): 'and he will judge the world in righteousness.' The noun tēbēl ('world, inhabited earth') expands the scope beyond Israel to the entire populated world. The prepositional pair bĕṣedeq and bĕmêšārîm ('in righteousness / in uprightness') function as ethical instruments: YHWH's judgment is characterized by covenant fidelity and impartial equity. This verse is a seed of the NT vision of Christ judging the world 'in righteousness' (Acts 17:31).
9: wîhî YHWH miśgāb laddāk (waYHWH miśgāḇ laddāḵ): 'and the LORD is a high refuge for the oppressed.' The noun miśgāb ('high fortress, stronghold') comes from śāgab ('to be high/inaccessible'); YHWH himself is the elevated place to which the poor flee. The rare noun dāk ('crushed one, oppressed') points to those who have been ground down by the wicked — the same social group the preceding verses described the wicked exploiting. The verb yihyeh (imperfect) indicates ongoing, present-tense provision: not a one-time rescue but a permanent refuge.
10: wĕyibṭĕḥû bĕkā yôdĕʿê šĕmekā (wĕyiḇṭĕḥû ḇĕḵā yôḏĕʿê šĕmeḵā): 'and those who know your name trust in you.' The verb bāṭaḥ ('trust, rely upon') in the qal expresses confident, settled reliance — not merely intellectual belief. The qualifier yôdĕʿê šĕmekā, 'those who know your name,' is covenant language: knowing the name in the OT means experiencing YHWH's revealed character (Exodus 3:14-15; Psalms 6:3). The result: kî lōʾ-ʿāzabtā dōrĕšêkā YHWH, 'for you have not forsaken those who seek you' — a theological anchor for unanswered prayer: seeking is never in vain.
11: zammĕrû laYHWH yōšēb ṣiyyôn (zammĕrû laYHWH yōšēḇ ṣiyyôn): 'sing praises to the LORD who dwells in Zion.' The piel imperative zammĕrû ('make music, sing') summons communal praise. The designation yōšēb ṣiyyôn, 'the one who dwells in Zion,' grounds YHWH's cosmic throne (Psalms 9:7) in a specific earthly locus — Zion/Jerusalem as the place where heaven and earth meet. The second half, haggîdû bāʿammîm ʿălîlôtāyw, 'declare his deeds among the peoples,' extends the witness of praise to the nations — a missionary impulse within the Psalter.
12: kî dōrēš dāmîm ʾôtām zākar (kî dōrēš dāmîm ʾôṯām zāḵar): 'for he who avenges blood remembers them.' The participle dōrēš dāmîm ('one who investigates/requires blood') draws on the legal language of blood-vengeance: YHWH as the one who holds killers accountable. The phrase lōʾ šākaḥ ṣaʿaqat ʿănāwîm, 'he does not forget the cry of the afflicted,' creates an inclusio with Psalms 9:9: the oppressed who flee to YHWH are never outside his attention. The noun ʿănāwîm ('afflicted, poor, humble') is a key Psalter term for those who trust wholly in YHWH (cf. Psalms 22:26; Psalms 37:11; Matthew 5:5).
13: ḥonnēnî YHWH rĕʾēh ʿonî mēśonĕʾay (ḥonnēnî YHWH rĕʾēh ʿonî mēśônĕʾāy): 'Be gracious to me, O LORD; see my affliction by those who hate me.' The qal imperative ḥonnēnî invokes the ḥānan root (grace, favor), used of the unmerited favor of a superior to an inferior. The verb rĕʾēh ('see/observe') is a plea for divine attention — the counterpart to lōʾ šākaḥ in Psalms 9:12. The phrase mǎrômmî miššaʿărê māwet, 'you who lift me from the gates of death,' introduces the gates of death (Sheol's threshold) as the extremity from which YHWH rescues.
14: lĕmaʿan ʾăsappĕrâ kol-tĕhillātekā (lĕmaʿan ʾăsappĕrâ ḵol-tĕhillāṯeḵā): 'that I may declare all your praise.' The purpose clause with the cohortative ʾăsappĕrâ ('I will tell/recount') shows that deliverance is not merely personal relief but has a doxological telos — it creates testimony. The setting is bĕšaʿărê bat-ṣiyyôn, 'in the gates of the daughter of Zion,' i.e., at the city gate where public life and worship converged. The pledge to praise publicly in the assembly is a votive form common in Psalter laments (cf. Psalms 22:22, 25).
15: ṭāb'û gôyim bĕšaḥat ʿāśû (ṭāḇĕʿû gôyim bĕšaḥaṯ ʿāśû): 'the nations have sunk in the pit they made.' The verb ṭābaʿ ('sink, be submerged') is used of sinking in mud or deep waters; applied to nations, it vivifies their sudden collapse. The theological principle is lex talionis retributive justice: bāreŝet zû ṭāmānû nilkĕdâ raglām, 'in the net they hid, their own foot has been caught.' This principle — the wicked falling into their own traps — appears throughout the Psalter (cf. Psalms 7:15-16; Psalms 35:8) and grounds confidence in ultimate justice.
16: nôdaʿ YHWH mišpāṭ ʿāśâ (nôḏaʿ YHWH mišpāṭ ʿāśâ): 'the LORD has made himself known; he has executed judgment.' The niphal nôdaʿ ('he was made known') means that YHWH's nature is revealed through his acts of judgment — theodicy is also theology. The phrase bĕpōʿal yādāyw nôqēš rāšāʿ, 'the wicked is snared in the work of his own hands,' echoes Psalms 9:15: the trap-maker becomes the trapped. The enigmatic annotation Higgaion Selah may indicate a meditative musical interlude, inviting reflection on this reversal.
17: yāšûbû rĕšāʿîm lišĕʾôl (yāšûḇû rĕšāʿîm lišĕʾôl): 'the wicked shall return to Sheol.' The verb šûb ('return, go back') implies that Sheol is the wicked's natural origin or destination — dust to dust, darkness to darkness. The phrase kol-gôyim šĕkēḥê ʾĕlōhîm, 'all the nations that forget God,' extends the verdict beyond Israel to gentile nations who suppress the knowledge of God (cf. Romans 1:21). This verse forms the negative counterpart to Psalms 9:10 — those who know the name trust; those who forget God perish.
18: kî lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššākēaḥ ʾebyôn (kî lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššāḵēaḥ ʾeḇyôn): 'for the needy shall not always be forgotten.' The word ʾebyôn ('poor, destitute') denotes material poverty and, in the Psalter's theology, those who depend entirely on YHWH for survival. The phrase lōʾ lānĕṣaḥ yiššākēaḥ is emphatically negated: the forgetting is not permanent. The second half, tiqwat ʿănāwîm tōʾbad lāʿad, 'the hope of the afflicted shall not perish forever,' uses ʾeḇed ('perish') — the same verb used for the wicked's destruction in Psalms 9:3 — now negated for the righteous. Hope is imperishable.
19: qûmâ YHWH ʾal-yĕʿōz ʾĕnôš (qûmâ YHWH ʾal-yĕʿōz ʾĕnôš): 'Arise, O LORD; let not man prevail.' The imperative qûmâ ('arise!') is a liturgical summons to divine action, common in Psalter prayers (cf. Psalms 7:6; Psalms 44:26; Psalms 74:22). The noun ʾĕnôš ('mortal man' — from the root implying frailty/sickness) emphasizes human finitude: it is absurd for frail humans to triumph over the eternal God. The phrase yiššāpĕṭû gôyim ʿal-pānêkā, 'let the nations be judged before you,' repeats the court language of Psalms 9:4-8 — the psalm ends where it began, in the courtroom of heaven.
20: šîtâ YHWH môrâ lāhem (šîṯâ YHWH môrâ lāhem): 'Put them in fear, O LORD.' The noun môrâ (from yārēʾ, 'to fear') connotes the dread that falls on enemies when God acts. The phrase yēdĕʿû gôyim ʾĕnôš hēmmâ, 'let the nations know that they are but men,' is the psalm's culminating reversal: those who thought themselves lords of history (Psalms 9:4-6) are exposed as ʾĕnôš — mortal, contingent, creature. The Selah punctuates the closing appeal for all humanity to recognize creatureliness before the creator.
